THOMAS D. BECK
ROBERT H. SMOCKIN

Office & Branch
Offices

TWO PARK PLAZA
SUITE 450
IRVINE, CA 92714
TELEPHONE 714/251-2800
TELECOPIER 714/251-2801

July 14, 1992

FEDERAL EXPRESS                                    FPPC

Mr. John Wallace
Staff Attorney
California Fair Political
   Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA. 95804-0807

                                                   JUL 15 1992

Dear John:

     As you suggested in our telephone conversation, I
am writing to you and the California Fair Political Practices
Commission for advice concerning whether this law firm's
recent pro bono work may trigger an obligation to file a
report under the Political Reform Act.  Please note that if
we are required to file as either a major donor committee or
a committee making independent expenditures pursuant to Cal.
Gov. Code §82013, we face a July 31 filing deadline.  In
addition to the questions below, we request your timely
guidance as to whether or not we should file in either
capacity pending the Commission's reply to this letter.

     Since February 1992, this firm has represented four
parties (The League of Women's Voters, California Common
Cause, Children Now, and California Homeless and Housing
Coalition) in a legal challenge to the statewide ballot
initiative known as the Government Accountability and
Taxpayer Protection Act of 1992 (the "Initiative").

     On behalf of our clients, we filed a petition for a
writ of mandate to prevent the placement of the Initiative on
the November ballot.  The petition challenged the Initiative
on the basis that it violated the provision of the California
Constitution that provides that initiatives "embracing more
than one subject may not be submitted to the electors."  Cal.
Const., art II, §8(d).  The California Court of Appeal

Mr. John Wallace
July 14, 1992
page 2

for the Third Appellate District denied the petition.  League
of Women Voters v. Eu, 92 C.D.O.S. 5404 (June 19, 1992)  (A
copy of the opinion is attached to this letter.)  Our clients
intend to appeal the decision to the California Supreme
Court.

     None of our clients in this matter were organized
specifically to challenge the Initiative.  Howard, Rice has
had no contact with the No On the Wilson Initiative Committee
or any other committee specifically organized to oppose the
Initiative during the course of this representation.  We did
not provide any legal services in cooperation with the "No"
committee or at the committee's behest or direction.

     The legal work was provided on a pro bono basis.
At our normal billing rates, the attorneys' and staff time,
filing fees, research fees and other related costs provided
by Howard, Rice in this matter are in excess of $40,000.  At
least one of the attorneys who worked on the challenge spent
more than 10 percent of his time on the proceeding during a
period of several months.  However, if the legal services we
provided were allocated evenly to all four clients, no single
client received as much as 10 percent of any employee's
compensated time during a single month.  See Letter from the
Commission to Mark Borenstein (May 8, 1989)

     Given the preceding background, we have the
following questions:

     1.   Should our pro bono services be considered a
          "contribution" for purposes of Cal. Gov. Code
          §82015?

     2.   Does the Commission consider Howard, Rice to
          be either a major donor committee contributing
          to a "committee" under Cal. Gov. Code
          §82013(c) or a committee making independent
          expenditures under §82013(b)?

     3.   What forms, if any, must we file?

     Again, we ask that you pay special attention to the
impending July 31 deadline for the filing of certain
statements under the Political Reform Act and inform us as
soon as possible whether we should file as a major donor
committee or a committee making independent expenditures
pending the Commission's consideration of our

Mr. John Wallace
July 14, 1992
page 3

questions. If you need any further information, please call
me or Steven Mayer of this firm.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

ROBERT B. LOWE

92-407

California Homeless
and Housing Coalition
926 J Street, Suite 422 • Sacramento, CA 95814
(916) 447-0390 • FAX (916) 447-0458 • HantsNet HN0046

July 22, 1992

John Wallace
Staff Attorney
California Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Dear Mr. Wallace,

I tried to reach you by telephone today. I am writing for advice on whether the California Homeless and Housing Coalition (CHHC), as a plaintiff in a pre-election challenge to Proposition 165, has reporting obligations under the Fair Political Practices Act.

You should have already received the enclosed July 14, 1992 letter from Robert Lowe of Howard, Rice, Nemerovski, Canady, Robertson & Falk. As Mr. Lowe stated in the letter, the legal services are being provided on a pro-bono basis. No employee of CHHC has devoted 10% his or her work time to anti-initiative activities, including the lawsuit. Aside from the reporting obligations of our attorneys, I wanted advice on whether CHHC has any reporting responsibilities for this activity.

Please call me if you need more information or if I need to rephrase my request. It is my understanding that if we do have to report, we must do so by July 31.

Thank you for your assistance.

Sincerely,

Beth Steckler for
CALLIE HUTCHISON
Executive Director

encl.

Southern California Office: 1010 S. Flower, Suite 500 • Los Angeles, CA 90015 • Tel: (213) 746-7690 • Fax: (213) 748-2432 • HandsNet HN0047

SUITE 413
SACRAMENTO, CA
9 5 8 1 4
916 • 441 • 2444

Los Angeles
1930 14TH ST
STA MONICA, CA
9 0 4 0 4
310 • 399 • 7444

[CHILDREN NOW logo]

July 31, 1992

FPPC
AUG -3 1992
[RECEIVED stamp - illegible]

Mr. John Wallace,
Staff Attorney
California Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

RE: Filing of Forms 410 and Form 419

Dear Mr. Wallace::

On behalf of the League of Women Voters, the California Homeless and Housing Coalition, Common Cause and my own organization Children Now, we are under protest filing Form 410 (Statement of Organization) and Form 419 (Recipient Committee Campaign Disclosure Statement). In order to conform to the Fair Political Practices Commission's (FPPC) regulations, which apparently require that our separate organizations be treated as one entity, we have given ourselves the fictitious name of the "Coalition to Protect the Single Subject Rule". This "entity" has no legal basis.

In early 1992, each of the organizations listed above, through separate processes, individually decided to sign onto a lawsuit opposing the pre-qualification of the Government Accountability and Taxpayer Protection Act (now Proposition 165) based upon its violation of the single subject rule. These actions were not made in concert. The lawsuit was handled pro bono by the law firm of Howard, Rice, Nemerovski, Canady, Robertson & Falk.

Last week we learned that our attorney had sent a letter to the FPPC for advice of the firm obligation to file a report for the value of those services. This of course, raised the issue of any obligation to file on the part of our organizations. We are therefore now filing upon the verbal advice of the FPPC staff.

Because we are still uncertain as to when we became obligated to file (if we indeed became obligated), we are requesting a waiver of any fees and penalties, should this timely filing alone not be adequate. We base this request upon the fact that: 1) we were unaware that pro bono legal services qualified as reportable receipts; 2) we have never received a bill for services or information regarding the value of those services (until we requested such this week for this filing); 3) we do not consider ourselves to be a recipient committee because we are, have remained and acted as separate organizations; and 4) no employee of any of the four organizations has devoted 10% of his/her time to the lawsuit. In a letter to the FPPC dated July 22, 1992, the CHHC requested advice regarding reporting responsibilities around the lawsuit (copy attached).

California Fair Political Practices Commission
Page Two


We understand that the FPPC will not make a formal decision until on or
about August 15, 1992. Because today is the filing deadline for this period, we
are making this timely filing should your decision be adverse to our position.

Sincerely,

Sandra Simpson-Fontaine
Sandra Simpson-Fontaine
Director, Political and Legislative Affairs

cc: League of Women Voters
California Homeless and Housing Coalition
Common Cause
Steve Mayer

perjury under the laws of the State of California that the foregoing is true and correct.

embedding.first_500_words: The letter is from the Law Offices of Howard Rice Nemirovski Canady Robertson & Falk, addressed to Mr. John Wallace at the California Fair Political Practices Commission. The firm is seeking advice regarding whether their recent pro bono work may trigger an obligation to file a report under the Political Reform Act. The firm represents four parties in a legal challenge to the statewide ballot initiative known as the Government Accountability and Taxpayer Protection Act of 1992 (the "Initiative"). The letter explains that they filed a petition for a writ of mandate to prevent the placement of the Initiative on the November ballot, challenging it based on the California Constitution provision that initiatives "embracing more than one subject may not be submitted to the electors." Mr. John Wallace July 14, 1992 for the Third Appellate District denied the petition. League of Women Voters v. Eu, 92 C.D.O.S. 5404 (June 19, 1992) (A copy of the opinion is attached to this letter.) Our clients intend to appeal the decision to the California Supreme Court. None of our clients in this matter were organized specifically to challenge the Initiative. Howard, Rice has had no contact with the No On the Wilson Initiative Committee or any other committee specifically organized to oppose the Initiative during the course of this representation. We did not provide any legal services in cooperation with the "No" committee or at the committee's behest or direction. The legal work was provided on a pro bono basis. At our normal billing rates, the attorneys' and staff time, filing fees, research fees and other related costs provided by Howard, Rice in this matter are in excess of $40,000. At least one of the attorneys who worked on the challenge spent more than 10 percent of his time on the proceeding during a period of several months. However, if the legal services we provided were allocated evenly to all four clients, no single client received as much as 10 percent of any employee's compensated time during a single month. See Letter from the Commission to Mark Borenstein (May 8, 1989) Given the preceding background, we have the following questions: 1. Should our pro bono services be considered a "contribution" for purposes of Cal. Gov. Code §82015? 2. Does the Commission consider Howard, Rice to be either a major donor committee contributing to a "committee" under Cal. Gov. Code §82013(c) or a committee making independent expenditures under §82013(b)? 3. What forms, if any, must we file? Again, we ask that you pay special attention to the impending July 31 deadline for the filing of certain statements under the Political Reform Act and inform us as soon as possible whether we should file as a major donor committee or a committee making independent expenditures pending the Commission's consideration of our Mr. John Wallace July 14, 1992 page 3 questions. If you need any further information, please call me or Steven Mayer of this firm. Very truly yours, ROBERT B. LOWE July 22, 1992 John
